method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is a small, uninhabited coral island in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, about 40 km (25 mi) south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W, in the central Line Islands, also known as the Teraina Islands.[2] The island is the exposed crest of an ancient coral-reef cap over a massive extinct volcano, and its center is a dried former lagoon containing deep guano deposits.[2] Its emergent land area is listed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as 1,273 acres, with submerged lands and waters within the three-mile refuge boundary totaling 36,214 acres.[3]

The island is low and mostly sandy, reaching a maximum elevation of about 7 m (23 ft).[2] Jarvis has a tropical desert climate with high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sunlight, scant rainfall of less than 1 m (40 in) per year, and no known natural freshwater lens.[2] Vegetation is sparse and consists mainly of bunch grass, vines, and low-growing shrubs, with no plants larger than shrubs.[2] These terrestrial habitats provide nesting, roosting, and shelter for seabirds, shorebirds, and numerous land hermit crabs.[2]

Jarvis supports more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] The island has been described as having 14 species of breeding seabirds, and Jarvis alone has been estimated to support about 1 million sooty terns.[1][3] Historic guano removal and invasive mammals, including cats and rats, had major environmental impacts on Jarvis and nearby Baker and Howland islands.[1] Following eradication of invasive mammals, seabird recovery on Jarvis included increases in most bird species and the return of both shearwater species.[1]

The marine ecosystem around Jarvis is exceptionally productive because of the combined effects of wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling of the eastward-flowing current around the island.[2] Shallow marine benthic habitats include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side.[2] The fringing reef has been described by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as a healthy coral community, reflecting the island’s remoteness and limited human influence.[3] Jarvis is considered an important site for studying long-term climate change and periodic phenomena such as the El Niño–Southern Oscillation.[3]

==History==
The first known European sighting of Jarvis Island occurred on August 21, 1821, when Captain Brown of the British ship *Eliza Francis* sighted the island; the vessel was owned by Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, for whom the island was named.[2][4] American whalers and other seafarers later noted guano deposits there, and the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed the island in 1840.[2] In 1857, Benson and Charles Judd took possession of Jarvis for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act, and Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary* subsequently surveyed and formally claimed it for the United States.[2][4]

Guano deposits on Jarvis were worked from 1857 until 1879, when the island was abandoned after substantial quantities had been removed.[4][5] The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis in 1889, but did not develop mining operations there; a 1906 lease to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London likewise did not lead to renewed exploitation.[4][5] The United States reoccupied and reclaimed the island in 1935, and President Franklin D. Roosevelt placed its control and jurisdiction under the Secretary of the Interior by Executive Order 7368 on May 13, 1936.[3][4][5] Japanese forces attacked Jarvis in 1942, forcing the evacuation of the island’s colonists.[1]

On June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and its territorial sea out to the 3-nautical-mile limit as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] The stated purpose of the refuge emphasized preservation of the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystem, with special attention to large seabird nesting colonies.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also made part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The refuge is administered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as part of the National Wildlife Refuge System, whose mission is to conserve, manage, and, where appropriate, restore fish, wildlife, plants, and their habitats for present and future generations.[2] Its stated purpose at the time of transfer to the Service was “the preservation of the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine,” with special emphasis on large seabird nesting colonies.[5] The original refuge boundary included the island and its territorial sea extending to the three-mile limit, comprising 1,273 acres of emergent land and 36,214 acres of submerged lands and waters, for a total of 37,487 acres.[5] The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service later described the refuge as encompassing 429,853 acres, including 1,273 terrestrial acres and 428,580 submerged acres.[2] The World Database on Protected Areas lists Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge as a marine and coastal protected area with a reported area of 1,744.87 square kilometres.[1]

Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge is managed within the boundaries of the Pacific Islands Heritage Marine National Monument, established in 2009.[2] A comprehensive conservation planning document for the refuge was prepared by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in 2008, addressing management alternatives, conservation actions, and environmental assessment for the refuge.[4][5] In assessing proposed access for amateur radio operations, the Service stated in a draft compatibility determination that such activity would not detract from the refuge’s core mission to protect fish, wildlife, plants, coral reef communities, and other resources associated with its tidal lands, submerged lands, and waters.[2] In August 2024, a U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service biology team visited the refuge as part of the Pacific Islands Heritage Marine National Monument program, marking the Service’s first trip to Jarvis Island in more than six years.[3]

==Transportation==
Transportation to Jarvis Island is limited to marine vessels capable of extended open-ocean voyages; the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service describes travel by ship as the only method of visiting the island.[3] The island lies about 1,263 nautical miles from refuge management staff in Honolulu, and the voyage to Jarvis takes about eight days on average, making distance and a lack of affordable, reliable transportation major operational constraints.[3] Access for official or permitted activities has been provided by NOAA ships, U.S. Coast Guard vessels and private charter vessels.[3] Scientific expeditions commonly visit Jarvis as part of wider cruises to remote central Pacific island refuges, including Baker, Howland and Johnston Island National Wildlife Refuges.[3] Refuge planning identifies the absence of field-support infrastructure, extreme environmental conditions, safety concerns and the logistical difficulty of landing as additional constraints on transportation and operations at Jarvis.[3] For permitted expeditions, fuel and other supplies brought ashore must be transported and stored under environmental safeguards, including approved containers, limits on on-island fuel storage and secondary containment.[1]

==Military==
In the mid-1930s, Jarvis was included in the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, a Department of Commerce program that placed U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker so that weather stations and possible landing fields could support military and commercial air routes between Australia and California.[1] A related strategic purpose was to reinforce U.S. claims to the islands as a check on Japanese territorial expansion in the eastern Pacific.[1] The colonists, later known as Hui Panalāʻau, were largely young men recruited from schools in Hawaii, including Native Hawaiian students, and more than 130 men from Hawaii lived on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker during the project.[1][2] Military personnel were associated with the colonization effort, as shown in contemporary photographs from Howland Island depicting colonists with unidentified military personnel.[2] Physical traces of earlier military-related activity remain around Jarvis, including short channels blasted through shallow fringing reefs to facilitate small-boat access during historical mining and military operations.[4] Refuge planning documents also identify possible hazards that may remain from human occupation during the island’s guano-mining and military eras.[3]
